.rork/history/main/00mrmz2a75000_dsdmhxqeu5aw3ygc61f0m_user.json:  "content": "# IVX REELS + DEALS — P0 UI MIGRATION, ROOT AUDIT, FIX, DEPLOY AND LIVE VERIFICATION

MODE: EXECUTE NOW
PRIORITY: P0
SCOPE: Native app Main module, Reels module, Landing page, Deal detail routes, media cards, deep links, API/data mapping and production deployment.

DO NOT RETURN A PLAN ONLY.
DO NOT RETURN NARRATIVE CLAIMS.
DO NOT MARK VERIFIED WITHOUT REAL LIVE PROOF.
DO NOT KEEP BOTH OLD AND NEW REEL CARD SYSTEMS ACTIVE.
DO NOT ASK FOR CREDENTIALS UNLESS a real runtime request proves missing, expired, revoked, 401 or 403.

==================================================
OWNER-APPROVED DESIGN DECISION
==================================================

KEEP this NEW immersive full-screen reel card design:

- Full-screen vertical video/image
- Investment and Active badges
- Property title and location
- ROI
- Minimum investment
- Minimum ownership
- Tokenized
- JV Deal
- Buyer/investment category buttons
- Like
- Comment
- Save
- Share
- Audio control
- View Deal
- Invest Now
- Smooth vertical reel scrolling

Use this new reel card in:

1. Main/Home module
2. Reels module
3. Native Android app
4. Native iOS app
5. Expo development build
6. Public landing page
7. Authenticated landing/feed page

REMOVE this OLD Instagram feed-style property card:

- Small rectangular card
- Header/avatar above the media
- Media constrained inside a post box
- Like/comment/share row under the media
- Large card footer with View Deal and Invest Now
- Duplicate card presentation on Main
- Duplicate card presentation on Landing

The old card must not remain hidden behind a feature flag, old route, fallback component, alternate breakpoint or stale mobile layout.

==================================================
CURRENT CONFIRMED BUGS
==================================================

BUG 1:
Main module still displays the old Instagram-style deal card.

BUG 2:
Landing page still displays the old deal/reel card.

BUG 3:
New full-screen reel design exists but is not used consistently across Main, Reels and Landing.

BUG 4:
Opening a displayed reel/deal can become stuck forever on:

“Loading Deal”
“Fetching investment details...”

BUG 5:
Deal display and Deal Detail are not fully connected end to end.

BUG 6:
Possible mismatch between:
- reel ID
- property ID
- investment ID
- deal ID
- slug
- route parameter
- API lookup key

BUG 7:
Possible duplicate components or separate data sources are creating inconsistent UI and behavior.

==================================================
PHASE 1 — DISCOVER EVERY OLD AND NEW CARD
==================================================

Search the complete repository for every component, route and reference related to:

- ReelCard
- ReelsCard
- DealCard
- InvestmentCard
- PropertyCard
- FeedCard
- InstagramCard
- HomeFeed
- MainFeed
- LandingFeed
- ReelsFeed
- ExploreFeed
- FullScreenReel
- ReelItem
- DealPreview
- ViewDeal
- InvestNow
- LoadingDeal
- Fetching investment details

Return the exact files discovered.

Identify:

1. Which component renders the approved new full-screen reel.
2. Which component renders the old Instagram-style card.
3. Every screen still importing the old component.
4. Every responsive/web variant.
5. Every fallback renderer.
6. Every feature flag controlling cards.
7. Every cached/stale card implementation.
8. Every route that opens Deal Detail.

Do not assume there is only one old card.

==================================================
PHASE 2 — CREATE ONE CANONICAL REEL CARD
==================================================

Create or designate one shared canonical component:

CanonicalInvestmentReelCard

It must support:

- native app
- native web
- public landing
- authenticated landing
- main feed
- reels feed

Use shared design tokens and shared business logic.

Required properties:

- reelId
- dealId
- propertyId
- investmentId
- slug
- title
- subtitle
- location
- mediaUrl
- mediaType
- posterUrl
- roi
- minimumInvestment
- minimumOwnership
- status
- category
- likeCount
- commentCount
- saveCount
- shareCount
- isLiked
- isSaved
- audioEnabled
- onOpenDeal
- onInvest
- onLike
- onComment
- onSave
- onShare

Do not duplicate business logic between Main, Reels and Landing.

==================================================
PHASE 3 — REMOVE OLD CARD END TO END
==================================================

Replace every old-card import with the canonical new reel card in:

- Main/Home
- Reels
- Landing
- Public feed
- Authenticated feed
- Search results where reel media is shown
- Property preview where reel style is intended

Delete or deprecate the old component only after confirming no active import remains.

Run repository-wide checks proving:

- zero production imports of old card
- zero route references to old card
- zero fallback references
- zero mobile breakpoint references
- zero web breakpoint references
- zero duplicated old markup

If an old card is still required for an unrelated admin screen, rename it clearly and prevent it from rendering in customer-facing Main, Reels or Landing.

==================================================
PHASE 4 — FIX DEAL OPENING AND INFINITE LOADING
==================================================

Audit the complete path:

Displayed reel
→ user taps reel / View Deal
→ route generated
→ route parameter parsed
→ API request
→ database lookup
→ response mapping
→ Deal Detail render

Test every possible lookup key:

- deal.id
- deal.slug
- property.id
- investment.id
- reel.id
- external ID
- UUID
- numeric ID

Choose one canonical route contract.

Recommended contract:

/deal/[dealId]

or

/investments/[dealId]

The same exact identifier must be used by:

- Main reel
- Reels module
- Landing reel
- View Deal
- Invest Now
- Share deep link
- Notification deep link
- Saved items
- Search results

Fix any route that sends one ID type while the destination expects another.

==================================================
PHASE 5 — LOADING, ERROR AND TIMEOUT BEHAVIOR
==================================================

The Deal Detail screen must never remain on infinite loading.

Implement:

1. Maximum request timeout.
2. Abort/cancel stale requests.
3. Retry button.
4. Back button.
5. Specific not-found state.
6. Specific unauthorized state.
7. Specific network-failure state.
8. Trace ID on backend failures.
9. Safe fallback when optional fields are missing.
10. Skeleton only while an active request is pending.
11. Automatic retry only for retryable failures.
12. No endless spinner after request completion.

Required states:

- LOADING
- LOADED
- NOT_FOUND
- UNAUTHORIZED
- NETWORK_ERROR
- SERVER_ERROR
- INVALID_ROUTE
- RETRYING

Never display “Loading Deal” indefinitely.

==================================================
PHASE 6 — DATABASE AND API AUDIT
==================================================

Audit actual tables/views used for reels and deals, including all relevant:

- properties
- investments
- deals
- jv_deals
- reels
- landing_deals
- media
- property_media
- investment_media
- saved_items
- likes
- comments

Verify:

- every displayed reel links to an existing deal
- every deal has a valid primary media item
- every media URL is valid
- no orphan reels
- no deleted deal still appears in feed
- no null route ID
- no duplicate deals
- status filtering is correct
- display order is correct
- public visibility is respected
- owner-only records are not public
- landing uses production data, not mock data
- app and landing read the same canonical records

Create a controlled audit report listing:

- displayed reel ID
- linked deal ID
- linked property ID
- route generated
- API status
- database row found
- detail render result
- PASS/FAIL

==================================================
PHASE 7 — REEL UX QA
==================================================

Test the canonical reel card for:

- vertical swipe
- smooth snap behavior
- autoplay active item only
- pause inactive item
- mute/unmute
- app background/resume
- image fallback
- video poster
- slow network
- missing media
- orientation changes
- Android back button
- iOS navigation
- tablet layout
- web mobile viewport
- desktop landing viewport
- no page jumping
- no layout shift
- no card height recalculation loop
- no overlapping bottom navigation
- no overlapping system navigation
- no clipped View Deal or Invest Now buttons

The reel must fit the usable viewport, accounting for:

- status bar
- safe area
- header
- bottom navigation
- Android navigation bar
- browser toolbar on mobile web

==================================================
PHASE 8 — BUTTON AND ACTION QA
==================================================

Test every action independently:

1. Tap anywhere intended to open deal.
2. View Deal.
3. Invest Now.
4. Like.
5. Unlike.
6. Comment.
7. Save.
8. Unsave.
9. Share.
10. Copy deep link.
11. Audio mute.
12. Audio unmute.
13. Category selector.
14. Tokenized.
15. JV Deal.
16. Buyer/Investor option.
17. Profile/company link.
18. Back from Deal Detail.
19. Return to exact previous reel position.
20. Refresh feed.

Acceptance:

- one tap produces one action
- no duplicate requests
- no duplicate likes
- no duplicate saved records
- no route to wrong property
- no loss of scroll position
- no infinite loading
- no silent failure

==================================================
PHASE 9 — ROOT-BY-ROOT DEAL TESTING
==================================================

Discover every deal route in the repository and test them individually.

For every route return:

- route path
- source screen
- identifier type
- expected destination
- actual destination
- API endpoint
- HTTP status
- response ID
- render result
- latency
- trace ID
- PASS/FAIL

At minimum test:

- Main → Deal
- Reels → Deal
- Landing → Deal
- Search → Deal
- Saved → Deal
- Portfolio → Deal
- Notification → Deal
- Shared deep link → Deal
- Direct URL → Deal
- Logged-out public route
- Logged-in member route
- Investor route
- Owner route
- Invalid ID
- Deleted ID
- Missing media
- Slow API
- API 401
- API 404
- API 500

==================================================
PHASE 10 — REGRESSION TESTS
==================================================

Add automated tests for:

- Main uses canonical new reel card
- Reels uses canonical new reel card
- Landing uses canonical new reel card
- old reel card has zero production imports
- reel ID maps to correct deal ID
- View Deal opens correct deal
- Invest Now opens correct investment flow
- invalid ID exits loading state
- API 404 displays not-found state
- API timeout displays retry state
- media failure displays fallback
- feed retains position after returning
- duplicate actions are prevented
- public landing hides restricted deals
- app and landing return the same deal data
- every seeded production reel resolves to a real detail record

==================================================
PHASE 11 — BUILD AND DEPLOY
==================================================

Run the real repository commands for:

- dependency install
- lint
- typecheck
- unit tests
- integration tests
- route tests
- reel UI tests
- deal detail tests
- production web build
- Android production build
- backend build
- production smoke tests

Fix all P0 and P1 failures related to this scope.

Then:

1. Commit to the owner-controlled repository.
2. Push to the production branch.
3. Deploy backend if API or routing changed.
4. Deploy landing page.
5. Produce updated Android APK/build if native code changed.
6. Confirm production SHA matches GitHub SHA.
7. Clear stale CDN/build cache where required.
8. Verify live app and live landing.

==================================================
MANDATORY LIVE VERIFICATION
==================================================

Use real production data and prove:

1. Main shows the new full-screen reel card.
2. Main no longer shows the old card.
3. Reels shows the new full-screen reel card.
4. Landing shows the new full-screen reel card.
5. Landing no longer shows the old card.
6. Casa Rosario opens successfully.
7. View Deal opens the correct Casa Rosario record.
8. Invest Now opens the correct investment flow.
9. No infinite “Loading Deal” screen.
10. Like/comment/save/share work.
11. Returning from Deal Detail restores the same reel.
12. Android real-device test passes.
13. Production web mobile test passes.
14. Production landing test passes.

==================================================
RELEASE GATE
==================================================

Do not mark VERIFIED unless all are present:

- GitHub commit SHA

.rork/history/main/00mrnd8wsh000_emvekej2g35mycgkk6gn8_user.json:  "content": "# IVX 100M SCALE READINESS — STRESS TEST, CAPACITY AUDIT, FIX, DEPLOY AND VERIFIED EVIDENCE

MODE: EXECUTE NOW
PRIORITY: P0 / ENTERPRISE SCALE
GOAL: Determine the real tested capacity of IVX and build a verified path toward supporting 100 million registered users.

IMPORTANT:
- Do not claim IVX supports 100M users without measured evidence.
- Do not run uncontrolled destructive traffic against production.
- Use a production-like staging environment first.
- Protect real users, real investor data, real payments and real Supabase records.
- Do not expose secrets.
- Do not ask for credentials unless a real runtime test proves missing, expired, revoked, 401 or 403.
- Return measurements, commands, logs and deployment proof—not narrative.

==================================================
DEFINITION OF THE 100M TARGET
==================================================

Separate these concepts:

1. REGISTERED USER CAPACITY
   Target: database and identity architecture capable of holding 100,000,000 user accounts.

2. MONTHLY ACTIVE USERS
   Measure projected load at:
   - 100,000 MAU
   - 1,000,000 MAU
   - 10,000,000 MAU
   - 100,000,000 MAU

3. CONCURRENT USERS
   Test progressively:
   - 10
   - 100
   - 1,000
   - 5,000
   - 10,000
   - 25,000
   - 50,000
   - 100,000 concurrent virtual users

4. REQUEST THROUGHPUT
   Measure sustained and burst traffic:
   - 100 requests/second
   - 1,000 requests/second
   - 5,000 requests/second
   - 10,000 requests/second
   - 25,000 requests/second
   - 50,000 requests/second

Do not interpret “100M users” as 100M simultaneous live connections.

==================================================
PHASE 1 — DISCOVER THE REAL ARCHITECTURE
==================================================

Audit and document:

- Native Expo app
- Landing page
- Owner AI chat
- Public API
- Private API
- Render services
- Supabase Auth
- Supabase database
- Supabase Realtime
- Supabase Storage
- Redis/cache, if present
- background workers
- queues
- cron jobs
- websocket/socket transport
- notifications
- media delivery
- CDN
- DNS
- GitHub deployment pipeline
- payment/investment services
- analytics
- logging and monitoring

For every component record:

- service name
- environment
- repository
- branch
- deployed SHA
- instance size
- replica count
- autoscaling configuration
- CPU limit
- memory limit
- connection limit
- timeout
- rate limit
- database pool size
- storage limits
- single point of failure
- estimated maximum capacity

==================================================
PHASE 2 — IDENTIFY EVERY HIGH-TRAFFIC ROOT
==================================================

Discover and test all real user-critical roots, including:

AUTH:
- register
- email verification
- phone verification
- login
- refresh token
- logout
- password recovery
- owner login

FEEDS:
- main feed
- reels feed
- landing feed
- pagination
- media metadata
- likes
- comments
- saves
- shares

DEALS:
- property listing
- deal detail
- View Deal
- Invest Now
- search
- filters
- saved deals
- portfolio

CHAT:
- create conversation
- send message
- load history
- realtime receive
- attachment upload
- image delivery
- reconnect
- message persistence
- Owner AI request

MEMBERS/INVESTORS:
- member list
- investor list
- profile
- search
- role lookup

MEDIA:
- image upload
- video upload
- thumbnails
- streaming
- signed URLs
- storage access

For each discovered route return:

- route
- HTTP method
- auth type
- database calls
- external provider calls
- cache behavior
- average payload
- current latency
- current error rate
- rate limit
- PASS/FAIL

==================================================
PHASE 3 — BUILD A SAFE LOAD-TEST ENVIRONMENT
==================================================

Create or verify a staging environment that mirrors production:

- same application code
- same runtime versions
- equivalent database schema
- equivalent indexes
- equivalent service topology
- production-like autoscaling
- synthetic users only
- synthetic deals
- synthetic conversations
- synthetic media metadata
- no real payments
- no real investor transactions
- no email/SMS spam
- isolated test storage bucket
- isolated test database/project when possible

Create synthetic datasets:

- 100,000 users
- 1,000,000 users
- 10,000,000 user capacity model
- 1,000,000 properties/deals model where feasible
- 10,000,000 feed interactions model
- realistic conversations and messages
- realistic media references

If generating the full dataset is too expensive, use database-size extrapolation with measured row size, index size and query plans. Clearly label extrapolated results versus actual executed tests.

==================================================
PHASE 4 — OBSERVABILITY BEFORE LOAD
==================================================

Before testing, enable measurable monitoring for:

- CPU
- memory
- event-loop delay
- process restarts
- request count
- requests per second
- p50 latency
- p90 latency
- p95 latency
- p99 latency
- maximum latency
- HTTP 4xx rate
- HTTP 5xx rate
- timeout rate
- retry rate
- database connections
- connection pool saturation
- slow queries
- lock waits
- deadlocks
- index hit rate
- cache hit rate
- queue depth
- job delay
- websocket count
- realtime disconnects
- storage upload latency
- AI provider latency
- external API failures
- cost per test stage

Every load-test run must have:
- run ID
- timestamp
- git SHA
- deploy ID
- environment
- dataset size
- test script version

==================================================
PHASE 5 — BASELINE TESTS
==================================================

Run baseline tests first:

1. Single-user functional test
2. 10 concurrent users
3. 100 concurrent users
4. 1,000 concurrent users

Workload mix:

- 25% feed reads
- 15% deal detail reads
- 10% search
- 10% authentication/session refresh
- 10% likes/saves/comments
- 10% member/investor reads
- 10% chat message operations
- 5% attachment metadata
- 5% Owner AI or AI-disabled representative task

Report:

- total requests
- successful requests
- failed requests
- p50/p95/p99
- requests/second
- CPU
- memory
- DB connections
- errors by route

Fix all baseline failures before continuing.

==================================================
PHASE 6 — PROGRESSIVE LOAD TEST
==================================================

Run progressive stages. Stop automatically if safety thresholds are exceeded.

STAGE A:
- 1,000 concurrent users
- 15 minutes

STAGE B:
- 5,000 concurrent users
- 20 minutes

STAGE C:
- 10,000 concurrent users
- 30 minutes

STAGE D:
- 25,000 concurrent users
- 30 minutes

STAGE E:
- 50,000 concurrent users
- 30 minutes

STAGE F:
- 100,000 concurrent users
- only after prior stages pass
- 30 minutes

Safety stop thresholds:

- error rate above 1%
- p95 above 2 seconds for normal reads
- p99 above 5 seconds
- CPU above 85% for 5 minutes
- memory above 85%
- database pool above 85%
- sustained queue growth
- repeated service restart
- data corruption
- duplicate financial action
- authentication instability
- realtime disconnect rate above defined threshold

Record the exact first breaking point.

==================================================
PHASE 7 — SPIKE, SOAK AND FAILOVER TESTS
==================================================

SPIKE TEST:
- increase traffic 10x within 60 seconds
- verify autoscaling
- verify no cascade failure
- verify rate limits
- verify recovery

SOAK TEST:
- run the maximum stable load for 8 hours
- check memory leaks
- connection leaks
- queue growth
- database bloat
- log growth
- storage errors
- degraded latency

FAILOVER TEST:
- restart one backend instance
- remove one instance
- restart worker
- simulate database connection interruption
- simulate Redis/cache loss
- simulate AI provider timeout
- simulate storage timeout
- confirm graceful recovery

DEPLOYMENT TEST:
- deploy during sustained test traffic
- verify zero or controlled downtime
- verify session continuity
- verify no message loss
- verify no duplicate requests

==================================================
PHASE 8 — DATABASE SCALE AUDIT
==================================================

Audit every high-volume table:

- users/profiles
- members
- investors
- properties
- investments
- deals
- reels
- likes
- comments
- saves
- conversations
- messages
- notifications
- transactions
- audit logs
- media

For each table:

- current row count
- projected 100M-scale row count
- average row size
- total projected storage
- index size
- primary key strategy
- partition strategy
- retention policy
- archive policy
- slowest query
- query plan
- missing indexes
- duplicate indexes
- RLS cost
- pagination method
- offset pagination risks
- cursor pagination readiness

Required fixes:

- cursor-based pagination for large feeds
- indexes for all frequent filters/joins
- no unbounded SELECT
- no N+1 query loops
- no full-table count on hot paths
- partition high-volume event/message tables when required
- connection pooling
- read replicas or equivalent plan
- archival strategy
- backup and restore test

==================================================
PHASE 9 — REALTIME AND CHAT SCALE
==================================================

Test:

- 1,000 websocket/realtime connections
- 5,000
- 10,000
- 25,000
- maximum supported by environment

Measure:

- successful connections
- reconnect rate
- dropped messages
- duplicate messages
- ordering failures
- message delivery p95
- persistence latency
- history load latency
- fan-out behavior
- server memory per connection

Acceptance:

- zero lost persisted messages
- zero duplicated message IDs
- deterministic ordering

API keys authenticate your requests to the AI Gateway. This page covers how to:

Text: # REST API Reference

For sending inference requests, the AI SDK is the easiest way to interact with AI Gateway. You can also send requests through the chat completions, responses, Anthropic Messages, or OpenResponses APIs.

AI Gateway exposes a REST API for looking up usage and generations, querying spend reports, and discovering models. This page is the canonical reference for those endpoints. The AI SDK AI Gateway provider also exposes TypeScript APIs for the same data, which you can use as an alternative to calling the REST endpoints directly.

## Base URL

```bash filename="Base URL"
https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1

```

## Authentication

Most endpoints require authentication. The exceptions are noted on each endpoint.

### API key (Bearer token)

Pass an AI Gateway API key in the `Authorization` header. AI Gateway infers the team from the key.

```http filename="HTTP header"
Authorization: Bearer <api_key>

```

### Vercel OIDC token

On Vercel deployments, you can pass the Vercel OIDC token in the `Authorization` header instead. The token is generated automatically per project.

The `vercel ai-gateway` command manages AI Gateway resources from the Vercel CLI, including API keys and routing rules.

```bash
vercel ai-gateway [subcommand]
```

```bash
vercel ai-gateway api-keys [subcommand]
```

Create a new AI Gateway API key. The CLI returns the plaintext key once; store it securely.

```bash
vercel ai-gateway api-keys create

```

Create an API key interactively, using the default settings.

```bash
vercel ai-gateway api-keys create --name my-key --budget 500 --refresh-period monthly
```

Create an API key with a human-readable name and a monthly $500 quota.

Text: # OpenAI Chat Completions API

AI Gateway provides OpenAI Chat Completions API endpoints, letting you use multiple AI providers through a familiar interface. You can use existing OpenAI client libraries, switch to AI Gateway with a URL change, and keep your current tools and workflows without code rewrites.

The Chat Completions API implements the same specification as the OpenAI Chat Completions API.

## Base URL

The Chat Completions API is available at the following base URL:

```
https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1

```

## Authentication

The Chat Completions API supports the same authentication methods as the main AI Gateway:

- API key: Use your AI Gateway API key with the `Authorization: Bearer ` header
- OIDC token: Use your Vercel OIDC token with the `Authorization: Bearer ` header

You only need to use one of these forms of authentication. If an API key is specified it will take precedence over any OIDC token, even if the API key is invalid.

## Supported endpoints

The AI Gateway supports the following Chat Completions API endpoints:

- `GET /models` - List available models
- `GET /models/{model}` - Retr

Title: OIDC
URL: https://vercel.com/docs/ai-gateway/authentication-and-byok/oidc
Text: # OIDC

The Vercel OIDC token is a way to authenticate your requests to the AI Gateway without needing to manage an API key. Vercel automatically generates the OIDC token that it associates with your Vercel project.

> 💡 Note: Vercel OIDC tokens are only valid for 12 hours, so you will need to refresh
> them periodically during local development. You can do this by running `vercel env pull` again.

## Setting up OIDC authentication

- Link to a Vercel project Before you can use the OIDC token during local development, ensure that you link your application to a Vercel project: vercel link

- Pull environment variables Pull the environment variables from Vercel to get the OIDC token: vercel env pull

- Use OIDC authentication in your code With OIDC authentication, you can directly use the gateway provider without needing to obtain an API key or set it in an environment variable: import { generateText } from 'ai';

export async function GET() {
 const result = await generateText({
 model: 'xai/grok-4.3',
 prompt: 'Why is the sky blue?',
 });
 return Response.json(result);
}
